questions.question: What are the three phases of the social anxiety cycle identified by researchers?

options.text: Trigger, panic, recovery
Anticipatory processing (before), in-situation processing (during), and post-event rumination (after)
Avoidance, exposure, habituation
Thought, feeling, behavior

questions.explanation: Researchers Clark & Wells (1995) and Rapee & Heimberg (1997) identified a three-phase cycle: anticipatory processing (worrying before the event), in-situation processing (self-focused attention during the event), and post-event rumination (replaying and critiquing afterward). Each phase feeds the next, creating a self-perpetuating loop.

questions.question: Why does self-focused attention during social situations actually create the outcome you fear?

options.text: Because focusing on yourself makes your physical symptoms visible to everyone
Because self-monitoring takes attention away from the conversation, making you seem less engaged — which is exactly what you fear
Because people can sense when you are self-conscious and they judge you for it
Because self-focus triggers a visible physiological response that others cannot ignore

questions.explanation: By focusing inward — monitoring your heart rate, checking for blushing, analyzing your words — you actually create the very outcome you fear. You seem less engaged because you ARE less engaged; your attention is on yourself rather than the conversation. Meanwhile, you miss friendly smiles and interested nods from others.

questions.question: What does research show about the relationship between post-event rumination and social anxiety?

options.text: Rumination helps people learn from social mistakes and improves future performance
Rumination has no measurable effect on social anxiety levels
A meta-analysis found a moderate correlation (r = .45) between post-event rumination and social anxiety severity, with rumination distorting memories more negatively over time
Rumination only affects people who have been diagnosed with social anxiety disorder

questions.explanation: A meta-analysis of 35 studies (over 10,000 participants) found a moderate correlation between post-event rumination and social anxiety severity. While rumination feels productive ('learning from mistakes'), it actually distorts memories to be more negative than reality, reinforces negative self-beliefs, and increases anticipatory anxiety for future events.

questions.question: When a social situation goes well despite your anxiety, how do safety behaviors prevent you from benefiting?

options.text: Safety behaviors make the situation go well, so there is nothing to learn
You attribute the success to the safety behavior rather than to reality, preventing you from learning you would have been fine anyway
Safety behaviors make the situation worse, confirming your fears
Safety behaviors cause other people to treat you differently

questions.explanation: When things go well after using a safety behavior, you think 'It was only okay because I avoided eye contact' or 'It was only fine because I had a drink first.' This prevents you from learning the most important lesson: you would have been fine anyway. The success gets credited to the crutch, not to your actual capability.

questions.question: Why is breaking the social anxiety cycle at any single point considered effective?

options.text: Because social anxiety only operates in one phase at a time
Because the phases are independent and do not influence each other
Because the three phases feed into each other, so disrupting one weakens the entire cycle
Because breaking one phase permanently eliminates social anxiety

questions.explanation: The social anxiety cycle is self-perpetuating — each phase feeds the next. Anticipatory anxiety increases self-focus during the event, which triggers post-event rumination, which worsens anticipatory anxiety for the next event. Because the phases are interconnected, breaking the cycle at any single point creates improvements across all phases.
